ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for poached egg; closest match is hen egg whole cooked (FOODON_03301710). Poaching method is not distinguished at this level.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked hen eggs broadly; no poaching-specific sub-facet available in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 is a broad food concept for eggs; no cooking-method-specific code for poached egg is available.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium poached chicken egg (approximately 50 g) is a typical single-serving portion, providing around 71 kcal, 6.3 g protein, and 4.7 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Poached chicken egg, whole — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for poached chicken egg, whole (cr_id F01060013). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01132 (poached egg) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat fractions.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for whole poached egg
gi_source.source_match_notes: Poached chicken egg contains negligible carbohydrates (0.71 g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable as the food does not raise blood glucose in a measurable way. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for eggs, consistent with standard practice for very-low-carbohydrate foods. GL per serving is set to 0.0 as carbohydrate load is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 297 mg/100g — borderline elevated; potassium (138 mg/100g) provides partial offset but ratio remains unfavourable
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit poached egg to 1–2 eggs per meal, avoid adding soy sauce or table salt, and account for the 297 mg/100g sodium within their total daily sodium budget of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrates (0.71 g/100g) and high protein (12.51 g/100g) — no meaningful glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Poached egg is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and high protein, which slows gastric emptying; however, those with concurrent dyslipidaemia should limit intake to 1 egg per day given the high cholesterol content of 370 mg/100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (370 mg/100g) and borderline sodium (297 mg/100g); saturated fat is moderate at approximately 2.8 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit poached egg to 1 egg per day to manage dietary cholesterol intake, choose poaching over frying to avoid additional saturated fat from cooking oils, and avoid sodium-rich accompaniments such as soy sauce or processed meats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Poaching avoids AGE formation; however, no positive anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants) are present in meaningful quantities
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Poached egg is preferred over fried egg for rheumatoid arthritis patients as poaching avoids advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation; limit to 1–2 eggs per day given high cholesterol (370 mg/100g), especially for those on corticosteroids affecting lipid metabolism.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A poached chicken egg is a whole egg cooked by gently submerging it in simmering water (approximately 80–90°C) for 3–5 minutes without the shell, resulting in a set egg white and a soft, runny yolk. Poaching is a low-fat cooking method that adds no oil or butter, making it one of the healthiest egg preparations. The texture is delicate — the white is tender and silky rather than rubbery, and the yolk is warm and liquid. Nutritionally, per 100 g the poached egg provides 143 kcal, 12.51 g protein, 9.47 g fat (of which approximately 2.8 g is saturated), 0.71 g carbohydrate, and 297 mg sodium. Cholesterol is high at 370 mg/100g. Dietary fibre is 0 g. The egg is a complete protein source containing all essential amino acids. It is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to negligible carbohydrate content, but cholesterol and sodium levels warrant moderation for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. Poaching avoids the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with high-heat dry cooking methods such as frying. The food is halal, gluten-free, dairy-free, and vegetarian but not vegan. It is widely consumed across global cuisines as a breakfast item, brunch staple, or protein component in salads and grain bowls.
common_user_queries: Is poached egg good for diabetes?
How many calories in a poached egg?
Is poached egg healthy for high blood pressure?
Can I eat poached egg on a keto diet?
Is poached egg better than fried egg?
Is poached egg suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01060013) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. The food identity is unambiguous and the preparation method is clearly defined.
notes: Saturated fat value of 2.8 g/100g is derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for poached egg (NDB 01132) as a cross-reference, since the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted value. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — eggs contain no dietary fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable: poached chicken egg contains negligible carbohydrates (0.71 g/100g); the University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a GI value to eggs, consistent with standard nutritional practice for very-low-carbohydrate animal-protein foods. The following fields are set to -1 because the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item (cr_id F01060013) does not individually report these values, and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01132) was consulted for cross-reference but values were not incorporated to preserve source integrity of the HPB primary record: monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fraction not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; polyunsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fraction not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; trans_fat_g — fatty acid fraction not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_c_mg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b6_mg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b12_mcg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; folate_mcg_dfe — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_d_mcg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_e_mg — micronutrient not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; phosphorus_mg — mineral not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; magnesium_mg — mineral not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; zinc_mg — mineral not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; selenium_mcg — mineral not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. The input record listed 'plant_based' as a dietary tag, which is incorrect for an egg product and has been removed. The food is not vegan. Kosher status is not confirmed and set to false pending rabbinical certification verification.

Expanded notes field to individually document every -1 sentinel field with explicit reason (not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry) and source consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01132), satisfying G3 numeric hygiene requirements.
Confirmed glycemic_index is JSON null (not numeric 0 and not string), satisfying Q4 glycemic completeness for negligible-carbohydrate foods per the critical rule.
